title: The divine comedy
author: Dante Alighieri
summary: Dante, lost in a dark wood midway through his life, encounters three beasts that block his ascent. He meets the Roman poet Virgil, who explains that Beatrice in heaven sent him as Dante’s guide. Virgil promises to lead Dante through the infernal regions—where despairing souls suffer—and then through Purgatory, until a worthier spirit may escort him to Paradise. Along the way Dante sees tormented spirits, hears prophetic woes, and learns moral and theological lessons about justice, divine judgment, and human failings, the journey framing a spiritual quest from fear and hope, redemption and enduring poetic imagination.

characters.name: Dante
characters.lifespan: living pilgrim who journeys through Hell and Purgatory in search of salvation

characters.name: Virgil
characters.lifespan: a shade from classical antiquity dwelling among the suspended in Limbo; guides Dante through Hell and Purgatory but cannot enter Heaven

characters.name: Beatrice
characters.lifespan: a blessed soul in Heaven who commissions Virgil to aid Dante

characters.name: Lucia
characters.lifespan: a heavenly spirit sent by Beatrice to assist Dante’s salvation

characters.name: Rachel
characters.lifespan: a blessed biblical figure encountered or referenced among heavenly attendants

characters.name: Aeneas
characters.lifespan: referenced as the classical traveler who visited the underworld; not an active presence in these excerpts

characters.name: Anchises
characters.lifespan: father of Aeneas mentioned as subject of Virgil’s poem

characters.name: Silvius
characters.lifespan: mentioned as progeny lineage (Silvius’ sire referenced)

characters.name: Camilla
characters.lifespan: named among famed fallen figures of Italy; referenced in prophecy

characters.name: Nisus
characters.lifespan: named as one who fell for Italy in classical tales

characters.name: Euryalus
characters.lifespan: named together with Nisus as fallen heroes

characters.name: Turnus
characters.lifespan: referenced as a famed figure in Italian epic tradition

characters.name: Panther
characters.lifespan: one of three allegorical beasts that impede Dante at the wood’s entrance

characters.name: Lion
characters.lifespan: second beast encountered, intensifying Dante’s fear

characters.name: She-wolf
characters.lifespan: third beast symbolizing avarice or incontinence; forces Dante backward

characters.name: God (Almighty King)
characters.lifespan: supreme divine authority who ordains order and judgment

characters.name: Saint Peter
characters.lifespan: referenced as associated with the gate and authority of the Church

characters.name: Paul
characters.lifespan: referenced as traveler to the heavens in scripture; used to contrast Dante’s worthiness

characters.name: Julius
characters.lifespan: referenced in historical context of Rome’s power

characters.name: Augustus
characters.personality: revered ruler in text’s historical scene
characters.lifespan: mentioned as the era during which Virgil lived

unusual_words.context: Opening line: 'In the midway of this our mortal life'
Dante describes himself: 'I found me in a gloomy wood, astray'
describes the wood: 'a gloomy wood'
the forest is called 'how savage wild'
describing 'how robust and rough its growth'
Dante's reaction: 'to remember only, my dismay Renew s'
'Forespent with toiling, ’scap’d from sea to shore'
Dante: 'Forespent with toiling, ’scap’d from sea to shore'
'My weary frame After short pause recomforted, again'
Dante meets 'a panther, nimble, light, And cover’d with a speckled skin'
describing the panther's skin: 'speckled skin'
'The hour was morning’s prime, and on his way Aloft the sun ascended with those stars' and 'the matin dawn'
used repeatedly, e.g., 'e'en to tell' and 'e'en so my spirit'
'Soon that joy was chas'd,' describing how hope is driven away
Dante is 'appall'd' at the sight of the she-wolf
Dante: 'I with front abash’d replied.' when meeting Virgil
describing the she-wolf's effect: 'many a land hath made Disconsolate ere now.'
describing the she-wolf: 'never sated is her ravenous will'
the beast described as 'so bad and so accursed in her kind'
prophecy: 'his land shall be The land ’twixt either Feltro.'
named in Virgil's prophecy: 'Camilla, virgin pure'
listed among fallen Italian figures: 'Nisus, Euryalus'
paired with Nisus in reference: 'Nisus, Euryalus'
referenced in Virgil's list of fallen heroes: 'and Turnus fell.'

unusual_words.definition: father of Aeneas in Roman epic (proper name)
unusual_words.context: Virgil introduces himself: 'I made Anchises’ upright son The subject of my song'
referenced: 'who came from Troy, When the flames prey’d on Ilium’s haughty towers.'

unusual_words.definition: ancient city; setting of the Aeneid
unusual_words.context: mentioned in recounting Aeneas' departure from Troy

unusual_words.definition: Julius (as in Julius Caesar) referenced historically
unusual_words.context: Virgil: 'when the power of Julius yet Was scarcely firm.'

unusual_words.definition: Roman emperor mentioned as chronological marker
unusual_words.context: Virgil: 'At Rome my life was past Beneath the mild Augustus'
Virgil is called 'A bard and made Anchises’ upright son The subject of my song'

unusual_words.definition: of Mantua; adjective form referencing Mantua (archaic form)
unusual_words.context: Virgil's origin: 'born of Lombard parents, Mantuana both By country'

unusual_words.definition: mythical name (proper noun); used in reference
unusual_words.context: Canto II mentions 'Silvius’ sire' when arguing worthiness
Virgil: 'I for thy profit pond’ring now devise'
the phrase 'heaven’s empyreal height' appears describing Heaven
Dante's invocation: 'O Muses! O high genius! now vouchsafe Your aid!'
Dante addresses Virgil as 'that shade magnanimous'
Dante calls: 'O Muses! O high genius! now vouchsafe Your aid!'
invocation: 'O Muses! O high genius! now vouchsafe Your aid!'
Dante: 'Seated with Rachel, her of ancient days'
Beatrice sends 'Lucia, of all cruelty the foe' to assist Dante

unusual_words.definition: Dante's beloved and heavenly intercessor (proper name)
unusual_words.context: Beatrice appears as the blessed lady who orders Dante's rescue
'Now was the day departing, and the air, Imbrown’d with shadows...'
'from their toils releas’d All animals on earth'
Dante refers to poetic excellence: 'Glory and light of all the tuneful train!'
Dante asks 'why is not thy succour lent To him...?'
Dante: 'So hast dispos’d me to renew my voyage, That my first purpose fully is resum’d.' and later 'as she will’d, thus am I come' in response to a behest

unusual_words.definition: great energy or enthusiasm in pursuit
unusual_words.context: Dante praises Virgil: 'with love immense Have conn’d it o'er. My master thou and guide!' showing zeal
Beatrice is called 'a dame, so blest And lovely'
refers to papal place: 'for the holy place, where sits Who to great Peter’s sacred chair succeeds.'
Dante: 'which my unerring memory shall retrace.'

unusual_words.definition: in the manner of an angel; divinely
unusual_words.context: Beatrice 'with gentle voice and soft Angelically tun’d her speech address’d'
Beatrice's speech 'Angelically tun’d her speech address’d'
Virgil asks 'wherefore to such perils past Return’st thou? wherefore not this pleasant mount Ascendest?'
Beatrice: 'Those things alone Are to be fear’d, whence evil may proceed'
Beatrice bids Virgil 'Speed now, And by thy eloquent persuasive tongue ... Assist him.' 'bid thee on this errand forth'
Frequent archaic address: 'Bard! thou who art my guide'
Virgil speaks to Dante: 'I commend him to thee.'
used throughout: 'Thy soul is by vile fear assail’d'
common archaic form: 'For every vein and pulse throughout my frame She hath made tremble.'
Virgil: 'He ... Adjudges me, and therefore hath decreed, That to his city none through me should come.'
reference: 'Sith he of Rome, and of Rome’s empire wide'
Lucia asks 'Dost thou not hear how pitiful his wail' regarding Dante

unusual_words.definition: a strong and fast-moving stream of water
unusual_words.context: image: 'the torrent flood, Swoln mightier than a sea, him struggling holds?'
'Swoln mightier than a sea' describing the flood
'whereat I felt Redoubled zeal to serve thee.'
Beatrice called 'a dame, so blest And lovely'

unusual_words.definition: a woman not married or sexually experienced; used honorifically
unusual_words.context: reference to Camilla: 'Camilla, virgin pure'
'Yet to discourse of what there good befell' opening line
Canto III inscription: 'Through me among the people lost for aye.'
'Such characters in colour dim I mark’d Over a portal’s lofty arch inscrib’d:'
'Such characters ... I mark’d Over a portal’s lofty arch inscrib’d:'
inscription: 'Through me you pass into eternal pain:'
'Justice the founder of my fabric mov’d: To rear me was the task of power divine, Supremest wisdom, and primeval love.'

unusual_words.definition: moral rightness and fairness; judicial principle
unusual_words.context: 'Justice the founder of my fabric mov’d:'

unusual_words.definition: to desert; here used in sense 'give up' (formal)
unusual_words.context: inscription: 'All hope abandon ye who enter here.'

Virgil: 'Thy soul is by vile fear assail’d'

unusual_words.definition: draws back in fear or disgust

'As one, who unresolves What he hath late resolv’d'
'What he hath late resolv’d' describing a vacillating person

unusual_words.definition: the soft light before sunrise or after sunset
unusual_words.context: 'some false semblance in the twilight gloom.'
Virgil promises Dante 'where thou shalt hear despairing shrieks, and see Spirits of old tormented'

unusual_words.definition: to call upon (a deity or power); to appeal to
unusual_words.context: 'Spirits of old tormented, who invoke A second death;'

unusual_words.definition: a celestial body; here symbolic of a guiding star
unusual_words.context: 'Already vested with that planet’s beam, Who leads all wanderers safe through every way.'
'Who leads all wanderers safe through every way.'

unusual_words.definition: a blood vessel; figuratively life or vigor
unusual_words.context: 'For every vein and pulse throughout my frame She hath made tremble.'

unusual_words.definition: rhythmic beat of the heart; figurative life
unusual_words.context: as above: 'For every vein and pulse throughout my frame'
Lucia addresses Virgil: 'O courteous shade of Mantua!'
Dante speaks of the 'high enterprise' he contemplates
'Into whose regions if thou then desire T' ascend, a spirit worthier than I Must lead thee'
'when in my Master’s sight I stand, thy praise to him I oft will tell.'
'While to the lower space with backward step I fell, my ken discern’d'
